title: Expose Keystone API via Gateway API with Envoy and nip.io
description:
# GitHub Issue #265: Expose the Keystone API via Gateway API in the Quick Start using Envoy Gateway and nip.io

**Category**: enhancement | **Scope**: Medium

**Description**: Take the optional `spec.gateway` HTTPRoute sub-reconciler landed in CC-0065 (#238, commit `0b2c7d9`) all the way through the Quick Start, so that a fresh kind cluster ends with the Keystone API reachable at a fixed, externally-looking hostname — `https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3` — instead of the current `kubectl port-forward svc/keystone-api -n openstack 5000:5000` workaround (`docs/quick-start.md:498-546`). Using the public `nip.io` wildcard resolver means any `*.127-0-0-1.nip.io` label resolves to `127.0.0.1` over regular DNS with no `/etc/hosts` editing, no MetalLB, and no CoreDNS customisation — the hostname is stable, identical on every developer's machine, and round-trips cleanly through the Keystone CRD's `status.endpoint` derivation (CC-0065: *"Derive status.endpoint from spec.gateway.hostname as https://{hostname}/v3 when gateway is set"*). Today `hack/deploy-infra.sh:707-729` installs the Gateway API `standard-install.yaml` CRDs but no controller, no `GatewayClass`, and no `Gateway`, so `spec.gateway` is effectively a prod-only code path the Quick Start never exercises. The HTTPRoute E2E (`tests/e2e/keystone/httproute/`) compensates by applying the stub CRD from `00-httproute-crd.yaml` and patching `Accepted=True` onto the parent status manually — a fresh onboarding user has no such shortcut.

Concrete steps: (1) add a kind-only Envoy Gateway install — matches the `envoy-gateway-system` namespace already referenced by `operators/keystone/api/v1alpha1/keystone_webhook_test.go:1083,1537`, the network-policy E2E (`tests/e2e/keystone/network-policy/00-keystone-cr.yaml:38`), and the CRD reference example at `docs/reference/keystone-crd.md:476`. Deliver it as a `HelmRepository` + `HelmRelease` under `deploy/kind/base/` (new `envoy-gateway.yaml`) so Flux reconciles it the same way it reconciles cert-manager / MariaDB-operator / memcached-operator (`deploy/flux-system/releases/*.yaml`); keep the production overlay `deploy/flux-system/` untouched — same kind-only posture as Headlamp (`deploy/kind/base/headlamp.yaml`) and the OpenBao UI flip (CC-0082). (2) Ship a `GatewayClass/envoy` + `Gateway/openstack-gw` (namespace `openstack`, not `envoy-gateway-system`, so Keystone's `spec.gateway.parentRef` does not need a `ReferenceGrant` — the operator explicitly does not manage `ReferenceGrant`, see `keystone_types.go:348-354`) with a single HTTPS listener on `:443`, hostname `keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io`, TLS terminated with a self-signed certificate issued by the existing `selfsigned-cluster-issuer` (already set up by the OpenBao chain). Use an `EnvoyProxy` CR to switch the proxy Service to `NodePort` with a fixed nodePort so `kind` can route it without `MetalLB`. Name the Gateway `openstack-gw` to line up with the E2E fixture at `tests/e2e/keystone/httproute/01-keystone-cr.yaml:40`. (3) Extend `hack/kind-config.yaml` with `extraPortMappings` mapping host `443` → the fixed Envoy NodePort so `https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3` (which resolves to `127.0.0.1` via the public nip.io wildcard) reaches the Envoy proxy on the kind container. (4) Add a `Step 2b` to `hack/deploy-infra.sh` — after the Gateway API CRDs install — that waits for the new Envoy Gateway `HelmRelease` and the `Gateway/openstack-gw` resource to report `Programmed=True`; extend the existing Step 4 HelmRelease wait list to include `envoy-gateway`. (5) Rewrite Quick Start Step 7 (`docs/quick-start.md:374-414`) so the sample `Keystone` CR includes `spec.gateway` with `parentRef.name: openstack-gw`, `hostname: keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io`, `path: /`. (6) Rewrite the `## Access Keystone from your local machine` section (`docs/quick-start.md:498-546`) to drop the port-forward and document: export `OS_AUTH_URL=https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3`, decide whether to trust the self-signed CA or set `OS_INSECURE=true`, then `openstack token issue` works without any terminal-blocking port-forward and without any local DNS / hosts-file editing. (7) Add an `## Accept the self-signed certificate` subsection explaining how to extract the self-signed CA from the `cert-manager` `selfsigned-cluster-issuer` and add it to the local trust store, for users who do not want `-k` / `OS_INSECURE`. (8) Update the `docs/reference/infrastructure/e2e-deployment.md:104-108` diagram to insert the Envoy Gateway + `Gateway/openstack-gw` install between `Step 2a` (CRDs) and `Step 3` (base overlay). (9) Add a new E2E suite under `tests/e2e/keystone/gateway-quick-start/` (or extend `httproute/`) that deploys against a real Envoy Gateway on kind and asserts that `HTTPRoute.status.parents[0].conditions[type=Accepted]=True` arrives from the real controller — not the simulated patch step — so the Quick Start path is covered by CI.

**Motivation**: CC-0065 (#238) made `spec.gateway` a first-class feature but kept the Quick Start on the port-forward path. That leaves three concrete gaps for the on-ramp: (a) contributors who read the CRD reference (`docs/reference/keystone-crd.md:590-615`) and decide to try `spec.gateway` in kind will set it, watch `HTTPRouteReady=GatewayAPINotInstalled` stick, and have no documented way to install a Gateway controller on the kind cluster — the operator reports `GatewayAPINotInstalled` as a terminal state if the `HTTPRoute` CRD is present but no Gateway controller has claimed the parent, exactly the kind-cluster state today; (b) the `status.endpoint` field, which the operator derives as `https://{hostname}/v3` when `spec.gateway` is set, is a hollow promise in the Quick Start — it prints a URL that nothing resolves; (c) the current port-forward flow forces a second terminal window and then silently breaks the moment `openstack` CLI tries to resolve catalog endpoints, because the catalog contains cluster-internal URLs (`docs/quick-start.md:541-545`) — a real Gateway listener fixes both problems at once for `identity`-scope commands and sets up the story for follow-on OpenStack services landing under the same Gateway. Envoy Gateway is the natural choice because the project already assumes that namespace (`envoy-gateway-system`) in tests and reference docs, and because the upstream Envoy Gateway Helm chart supports `EnvoyProxy`-based NodePort exposure out of the box — no `MetalLB` dependency. `nip.io` is chosen over `/etc/hosts` entries because it needs zero local configuration: every developer hits the exact same hostname, CI gets the same hostname, the Quick Start has no "add this line to your hosts file as root" caveat, and the Keystone CR fixtures are byte-for-byte reproducible across machines. Shape and scope match CC-0082 (OpenBao UI, kind-only Flux release + Quick Start section) and CC-0086/#257 (flux-operator Web UI, kind-only Flux release + Quick Start section): one kind-only manifest, one Quick Start rewrite, one E2E suite extension; production overlay untouched.

**Affected Areas**:
- `deploy/kind/base/envoy-gateway.yaml` (new — `HelmRepository` + `HelmRelease` for the upstream `envoy-gateway` chart, pinned; `EnvoyProxy` CR switching the proxy Service to `NodePort` with a fixed nodePort so `extraPortMappings` can target it)
- `deploy/kind/base/openstack-gateway.yaml` (new — `GatewayClass/envoy` (controllerName `gateway.envoyproxy.io/gatewayclass-controller`), `Gateway/openstack-gw` in namespace `openstack` with an HTTPS listener on `:443` for hostname `keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io`, `tls.mode: Terminate`, `certificateRefs` pointing at a `Certificate` issued by `selfsigned-cluster-issuer`)
- `deploy/kind/base/kustomization.yaml` (add the two new manifests to `resources` alongside `headlamp.yaml`; production overlay `deploy/flux-system/kustomization.yaml` stays unchanged)
- `deploy/flux-system/sources/` (new `envoy-gateway-charts.yaml` `HelmRepository` if the chart is not already reachable via `c5c3-charts`)
- `hack/kind-config.yaml` (add `extraPortMappings` for host `443` → container NodePort; the current file has an empty `nodes:` list so this is a single additive block)
- `hack/deploy-infra.sh` (new `Step 2b` that waits for `HelmRelease/envoy-gateway` Ready and `Gateway/openstack-gw` `Programmed=True`; extend the existing Step 4 HelmRelease wait list to include `envoy-gateway`)
- `docs/quick-start.md` — multiple edits: (a) Step 3 "What happens" table (`:103-116`) gets a `Step 2b` row for Envoy Gateway + Gateway install; (b) Step 7 sample CR (`:380-410`) gains `spec.gateway`; (c) the `## Access Keystone from your local machine` section (`:498-546`) is rewritten to use `https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3` directly — no `/etc/hosts` step, no port-forward; (d) a new subsection documents how to trust / bypass the self-signed TLS cert; (e) a short one-liner explains how `nip.io` works (public wildcard DNS that resolves `*.127-0-0-1.nip.io` to `127.0.0.1`, so no local DNS changes are required — the hostname is the same everywhere); (f) the architecture snapshot (`:141-155`) lists `envoy-gateway-system   envoy-gateway-*   Ready` alongside the existing controllers
- `docs/reference/infrastructure/e2e-deployment.md` (insert a Gateway-install block between the existing `Step 2a` and `Step 3` at `:104-108`)
- `docs/reference/keystone-crd.md:590-615` (add a kind-specific note pointing at the new Quick Start section and clarifying that `status.endpoint = https://{hostname}/v3` now actually resolves on a Quick Start cluster)
- `tests/e2e/keystone/gateway-quick-start/` (new Chainsaw suite that deploys a Keystone CR with `spec.gateway` against the real Envoy Gateway and asserts real `Accepted=True` from the controller, not a simulated patch)
- `renovate.json` (pin the Envoy Gateway chart version with a `customManagers` entry mirroring the `FLUX_OPERATOR_VERSION` pattern introduced by CC-0085, so chart bumps auto-PR)
- Nothing in `operators/keystone/` — `spec.gateway`, the webhook, the reconciler, and `HTTPRouteReady` are already in place from CC-0065

**Acceptance Criteria**:
- [ ] `deploy/kind/base/envoy-gateway.yaml` ships a `HelmRelease/envoy-gateway` in `envoy-gateway-system` that reaches `Ready=True` during `make deploy-infra` within the existing `HELMRELEASE_TIMEOUT` window
- [ ] `deploy/kind/base/openstack-gateway.yaml` ships a `GatewayClass/envoy` and a `Gateway/openstack-gw` in namespace `openstack`; on a fresh `make deploy-infra` run, `kubectl get gateway openstack-gw -n openstack -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Programmed")].status}'` returns `True`
- [ ] `hack/kind-config.yaml` exposes the Envoy proxy NodePort on host `443` via `extraPortMappings` so `https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3` is reachable on the developer's machine with no further port-forward
- [ ] The Quick Start Step 7 sample CR sets `spec.gateway` with `parentRef.name: openstack-gw`, `hostname: keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io`, `path: /`
- [ ] `status.endpoint` on the Quick Start CR reports `https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3` after reconciliation, matching `spec.gateway.hostname`
- [ ] `curl -k https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3` returns HTTP 200 with a `{"version": {"id": "v3", ...}}` JSON body on a freshly deployed kind cluster — no `/etc/hosts` edit required, no `kubectl port-forward` running
- [ ] With `OS_AUTH_URL=https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3`, `openstack token issue` succeeds — no `kubectl port-forward`, no DNS / hosts-file editing
- [ ] `docs/quick-start.md` `## Access Keystone from your local machine` section is rewritten and no longer references `kubectl port-forward svc/keystone-api`; the self-signed TLS handling (trust the CA or pass `-k` / `OS_INSECURE`) is documented inline; a short note explains the `nip.io` wildcard so readers understand why no local DNS config is needed
- [ ] `docs/reference/infrastructure/e2e-deployment.md` diagram (`:104-108` block) shows the Gateway install immediately after the Gateway API CRDs step
- [ ] `tests/e2e/keystone/gateway-quick-start/` asserts `HTTPRoute.status.parents[0].conditions[?(@.type=="Accepted")].status=True` arrives from the real Envoy Gateway controller within the Chainsaw timeout envelope, without any manual status patching
- [ ] `renovate.json` has a `customManagers` entry that pins the Envoy Gateway chart version with `minimumReleaseAge: "3 days"` and disabled major bumps, same pattern as `FLUX_OPERATOR_VERSION`
- [ ] `deploy/flux-system/kustomization.yaml`, `deploy/flux-system/fluxinstance.yaml`, and `deploy/flux-system/releases/*` are **not** modified — production overlay posture is unchanged
- [ ] The existing `kubectl port-forward svc/keystone-api` path still works for users on networks that block `nip.io` resolution; it is moved into a "Fallback" subsection but not deleted

**Non-Goals**:
- Installing Envoy Gateway (or any Gateway controller) in the production `deploy/flux-system/` overlay. The operator stays platform-agnostic — customers pick their own Gateway implementation, exactly as the CRD reference already documents (`docs/reference/keystone-crd.md:316-318`: *"The Gateway and GatewayClass are infrastructure concerns managed outside the operator"*). This feature is strictly a kind-overlay demo convenience, same posture as CC-0082 (OpenBao UI) and CC-0086 (Flux Web UI).
- Replacing the port-forward path for the rest of the Quick Start (Headlamp, OpenBao UI). Those stay on `kubectl port-forward` because they serve cluster-internal HTTP on non-443 ports; exposing them through the Gateway would require per-service HTTPRoute objects that do not match their current lifecycle.
- Cross-namespace `parentRef` support. `Gateway/openstack-gw` lives in the same `openstack` namespace as the Keystone CR to avoid `ReferenceGrant` — the operator explicitly does not manage `ReferenceGrant` (`keystone_types.go:348-354`: *"Cross-namespace references require a ReferenceGrant in the target namespace (out of scope for this operator)"*).
- Bringing up `MetalLB` in kind. Envoy Gateway's `EnvoyProxy` CR supports `NodePort` directly, which is the lighter-weight path and matches kind's single-node posture.
- Switching the E2E httproute suite from its simulated parent-status patch (`tests/e2e/keystone/httproute/chainsaw-test.yaml:99-100`) to the real controller. That suite exists to exercise the operator reconciler in isolation — a new suite under `gateway-quick-start/` covers the real-controller path instead.
- Editing `/etc/hosts` or shipping CoreDNS rewrite rules. The explicit design choice is public-wildcard DNS (`nip.io`) so the Quick Start has zero host-side DNS configuration. If `nip.io` is unreachable on a developer's network, the retained port-forward fallback covers that case.
- Wiring OIDC / SSO / real CAs on the kind Gateway. The Quick Start cluster is single-user localhost-only; self-signed is correct, matching the OpenBao UI's posture (`docs/quick-start.md:242-245`: *"Your browser will warn that the certificate is not trusted — this is expected for a kind cluster"*).

**References**:
- CC-0065 / #238 / commit `0b2c7d9` — the HTTPRoute sub-reconciler and `spec.gateway` field
- CRD reference for `spec.gateway`: `docs/reference/keystone-crd.md:590-636`
- Existing `spec.gateway` E2E fixture: `tests/e2e/keystone/httproute/01-keystone-cr.yaml`
- Envoy Gateway docs: https://gateway.envoyproxy.io/docs/
- Envoy Gateway `EnvoyProxy` NodePort pattern: https://gateway.envoyproxy.io/docs/tasks/operations/customize-envoyproxy/
- nip.io wildcard DNS: https://nip.io/
- Prior art for kind-only Flux releases: `deploy/kind/base/headlamp.yaml`, #246 (OpenBao UI, CC-0082), #257 (Flux Web UI, CC-0086)

**Labels:** enhancement


**Labels:** enhancement

---
Source: https://github.com/C5C3/forge/issues/265

stories.title: Quick Start user reaches Keystone at a stable HTTPS URL without a port-forward (CC-0088)
stories.role: new contributor running the Quick Start
stories.want: to reach the Keystone API at a fixed, externally-looking URL (https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3) on a fresh kind cluster
stories.so_that: I can run openstack CLI commands and curl checks without a blocking kubectl port-forward in a second terminal and without editing /etc/hosts or installing MetalLB
criteria: After `make deploy-infra` completes, `curl -k https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3` returns HTTP 200 with `{"version": {"id": "v3", ...}}` without any port-forward running
With `OS_AUTH_URL=https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3` exported, `openstack token issue` returns a valid token table
No `/etc/hosts` edit, no CoreDNS override, and no MetalLB install is required anywhere in the flow
The existing kubectl port-forward path remains available as a documented fallback under a `Fallback` subsection, with its commands still accurate

stories.title: Keystone CRD `spec.gateway` code path is exercised end-to-end on the Quick Start cluster (CC-0088)
stories.want: the sample Keystone CR in the Quick Start to populate `spec.gateway` and have the operator drive a real HTTPRoute through a real Gateway controller
stories.so_that: `status.endpoint` prints a URL that actually resolves, `HTTPRouteReady` flips to True from a real controller (not a simulated patch), and I can trust the feature before adopting it in production
criteria: Quick Start Step 7 sample CR sets `spec.gateway.parentRef.name: openstack-gw`, `spec.gateway.hostname: keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io`, `spec.gateway.path: /`
After reconciliation, `kubectl get keystone keystone -n openstack -o jsonpath='{.status.endpoint}'` returns `https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3`
`kubectl get keystone keystone -n openstack -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="HTTPRouteReady")].status}'` returns `True` with reason `HTTPRouteAccepted`
The operator's `GatewayAPINotInstalled` terminal state is never observed on the Quick Start cluster

stories.title: Kind-only Envoy Gateway install reconciles through Flux without touching the production overlay (CC-0088)
stories.want: Envoy Gateway (HelmRelease + GatewayClass + Gateway + EnvoyProxy) to be delivered only via `deploy/kind/base/`
stories.so_that: the production `deploy/flux-system/` overlay stays platform-agnostic, matching the posture documented in the CRD reference and the precedent set by CC-0082 (OpenBao UI) and CC-0086 (Flux Web UI)
criteria: `deploy/kind/base/envoy-gateway.yaml` ships a `HelmRepository` and `HelmRelease/envoy-gateway` in namespace `envoy-gateway-system` plus an `EnvoyProxy` CR switching the proxy Service to `NodePort` with a fixed nodePort

`deploy/kind/base/openstack-gateway.yaml` ships `GatewayClass/envoy` (controllerName `gateway.envoyproxy.io/gatewayclass-controller`), a cert-manager `Certificate` issued by `selfsigned-cluster-issuer`, and `Gateway/openstack-gw` in namespace `openstack` with a TLS listener on `:443` for hostname `keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io`
`deploy/kind/base/kustomization.yaml` references both new manifests under `resources` alongside `headlamp.yaml` and `flux-web.yaml`
`git diff main -- deploy/flux-system/` shows zero changes after the feature lands

stories.title: Deployment script waits for Gateway readiness and fails fast on regressions (CC-0088)
stories.want: `hack/deploy-infra.sh` to gate progress on `HelmRelease/envoy-gateway` Ready and `Gateway/openstack-gw` Programmed=True
stories.so_that: a broken Envoy Gateway install surfaces immediately with diagnostics instead of a silent Quick Start URL that never resolves
criteria: Step 4 HelmRelease wait list includes `envoy-gateway` and the script dumps HelmRelease status on timeout
A new `wait_for_gateway_programmed` (or equivalent) call polls `.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Programmed")].status == True` on `Gateway/openstack-gw -n openstack` within `HELMRELEASE_TIMEOUT`
On timeout the script dumps `kubectl describe gateway/openstack-gw -n openstack` and Envoy Gateway pod logs before exiting 1
Unit tests under `tests/unit/hack/` exercise the new wait function against a fake `kubectl` shim the same way `deploy_infra_reconcile_sources_test.sh` does

stories.title: Quick Start reader understands how nip.io and self-signed TLS work (CC-0088)
stories.want: the `Access Keystone from your local machine` section rewritten with a brief explanation of the nip.io public-wildcard DNS and clear instructions for either trusting the self-signed CA or passing `-k` / `OS_INSECURE=true`
stories.so_that: I am not surprised by certificate warnings and I understand why no local DNS configuration is needed
criteria: The section no longer references `kubectl port-forward svc/keystone-api` in its primary path; the port-forward instructions move to a `Fallback` subsection that explicitly says when to use it (e.g. networks that block nip.io)
A 1–2 sentence explainer covers how nip.io resolves `*.127-0-0-1.nip.io` to `127.0.0.1` over public DNS
An `## Accept the self-signed certificate` subsection documents extracting the CA from the `selfsigned-cluster-issuer` chain or using `OS_INSECURE=true` / `curl -k`
The `What happens` table in Step 3 gets a new row for the Envoy Gateway + Gateway install, and the architecture snapshot lists `envoy-gateway-system   envoy-gateway-*   Ready`

stories.title: CI validates the real-controller Gateway path so regressions fail in pipelines (CC-0088)
stories.want: a dedicated Chainsaw suite under `tests/e2e/keystone/gateway-quick-start/` that deploys a Keystone CR with `spec.gateway` against the real Envoy Gateway on kind
stories.so_that: the Quick Start Gateway path is exercised by CI without any simulated status patches, and any regression in Envoy Gateway, the EnvoyProxy NodePort config, or the Keystone operator's HTTPRoute reconciler is caught before release
criteria: Suite asserts `HTTPRoute.status.parents[0].conditions[?(@.type=="Accepted")].status == True` from the real controller (no `kubectl patch httproute --subresource=status`)
Suite asserts the Keystone CR's `HTTPRouteReady` condition reaches `status=True`, `reason=HTTPRouteAccepted`
Suite asserts `status.endpoint == https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3`
Suite is tagged with the `overlay: kind` label and gated by a presence probe (matching `flux-web-health` precedent) so non-kind runs skip cleanly

stories.title: Envoy Gateway chart version is pinned and Renovate-managed (CC-0088)
stories.want: the Envoy Gateway chart version pinned with a `customManagers` entry in `renovate.json` matching the `FLUX_OPERATOR_VERSION` pattern
stories.so_that: chart bumps are auto-PR'd with a release-age buffer, major bumps are held for human review, and the pinning location is discoverable for future upgrades
criteria: `renovate.json` gains a `customManagers` entry targeting `deploy/kind/base/envoy-gateway.yaml` that extracts the chart version
A matching `packageRules` entry sets `minimumReleaseAge: "3 days"` and disables major-version bumps
The version string is in one file only (the HelmRelease manifest or a dedicated constant) — not duplicated across the repo
A lint or dry-run of Renovate (documented in the task or in `scripts/`) confirms the new manager is discoverable

stories.title: Docs cross-reference the Quick Start Gateway path from reference material (CC-0088)
stories.want: `docs/reference/keystone-crd.md` and `docs/reference/infrastructure/e2e-deployment.md` updated to point at the new Quick Start Gateway section
stories.so_that: I can navigate from the `spec.gateway` field description to a working example on kind and understand where Envoy Gateway sits in the deploy sequence
criteria: `docs/reference/keystone-crd.md` gains a `kind-specific note` near the `Example — Basic Gateway Exposure` block that links to the new Quick Start subsection and clarifies `status.endpoint` resolves on kind

`docs/reference/infrastructure/e2e-deployment.md` ASCII deployment sequence diagram inserts an `Install Envoy Gateway + Gateway/openstack-gw` block between the existing `Install Gateway API standard CRDs` and `Step 3`
All new cross-references include the Feature ID `CC-0088` in commit messages and doc anchors where applicable
Existing doc examples in `docs/guides/advanced-configuration.md:139` remain unchanged (production posture unaffected)

requirements.description: The kind overlay SHALL install Envoy Gateway via a Flux `HelmRepository` + `HelmRelease` in namespace `envoy-gateway-system`, with chart version pinned and chart deployed kind-only.
requirements.rationale: Consistency with other Flux-reconciled controllers (cert-manager, mariadb-operator, memcached-operator) and kind-only demo posture (Headlamp, OpenBao UI, Flux Web UI) keep production free of the controller.

scenarios.name: HelmRelease reaches Ready on fresh kind
scenarios.when: `make deploy-infra` runs on a fresh kind cluster
scenarios.then: `kubectl get helmrelease envoy-gateway -n envoy-gateway-system -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].status}'` returns `True` within `HELMRELEASE_TIMEOUT`
and_then: Step 4 of the deploy script includes `envoy-gateway` in its wait list
Envoy Gateway controller pods are Running and Ready in `envoy-gateway-system`

scenarios.name: Production overlay unaffected
scenarios.when: `kubectl apply -k deploy/flux-system/` is applied to a production-like cluster
scenarios.then: no `envoy-gateway` HelmRelease is created and no `envoy-gateway-system` namespace is reconciled
and_then: `git diff main -- deploy/flux-system/` shows zero changes after the feature lands

scenarios.name: Chart source reachable
scenarios.when: the `envoy-gateway` HelmRelease reconciles
scenarios.then: its chart source (either a new `HelmRepository` under `deploy/flux-system/sources/envoy-gateway-charts.yaml` or the existing `c5c3-charts` OCI repo) resolves and the chart artifact is fetched
and_then: `kubectl get helmrepository -n flux-system` shows the source Ready=True

requirements.description: The kind overlay SHALL configure the Envoy proxy data-plane Service as `NodePort` via an `EnvoyProxy` CR with a fixed nodePort so that `hack/kind-config.yaml` `extraPortMappings` can target it deterministically.
requirements.rationale: Kind has no LoadBalancer controller by default; the single-node `EnvoyProxy`+`NodePort`+`extraPortMappings` chain is lighter than MetalLB and matches the project's single-node posture.

scenarios.name: Proxy Service is NodePort with fixed port
scenarios.when: Envoy Gateway reconciles `Gateway/openstack-gw`
scenarios.then: the generated proxy Service is of type `NodePort` and the HTTPS listener maps to a fixed `nodePort` (e.g. `31443`) declared in the `EnvoyProxy` CR
and_then: `kubectl get svc -n envoy-gateway-system -l gateway.envoyproxy.io/owning-gateway-name=openstack-gw` confirms the NodePort and port number

scenarios.name: Host port mapping works
scenarios.when: the kind cluster is created with the updated `hack/kind-config.yaml`
scenarios.then: the kind container maps host TCP `443` to the container's Envoy NodePort
and_then: A TLS connection from the host to `https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/` traverses the host:443 → kind container:NodePort path

scenarios.name: EnvoyProxy CR attached to GatewayClass
scenarios.when: the `GatewayClass/envoy` resource is created
scenarios.then: its `spec.parametersRef` references the EnvoyProxy CR in the correct namespace and the EnvoyProxy reconciler accepts the ref
and_then: `GatewayClass/envoy` status reports `Accepted=True`

requirements.description: The kind overlay SHALL provision `GatewayClass/envoy` and `Gateway/openstack-gw` (namespace `openstack`) with a single HTTPS listener on port `443`, hostname `keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io`, `tls.mode: Terminate`, and `certificateRefs` pointing to a cert-manager `Certificate` issued by `selfsigned-cluster-issuer`.
requirements.rationale: Same-namespace Gateway avoids `ReferenceGrant` (out of scope for the operator). Hostname aligns with nip.io wildcard resolution to `127.0.0.1`. Self-signed CA matches OpenBao's existing posture.

scenarios.name: Gateway Programmed=True
scenarios.when: Envoy Gateway finishes reconciling `Gateway/openstack-gw`
scenarios.then: `.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Programmed")].status` returns `True`
and_then: `.status.listeners[0].conditions[?(@.type=="Accepted")].status == True`
`.status.addresses[0]` is populated

scenarios.name: TLS Certificate materialised
scenarios.when: cert-manager reconciles the `Certificate` for `keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io`
scenarios.then: the referenced Secret exists in the Gateway's namespace and contains `tls.crt` + `tls.key`
and_then: `Certificate.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Ready")].status == True`

scenarios.name: Gateway in openstack namespace
scenarios.when: `kubectl get gateway openstack-gw -A`
scenarios.then: the Gateway is located in the `openstack` namespace, matching the Keystone CR's namespace
and_then: No `ReferenceGrant` objects are required for the Keystone CR's `spec.gateway.parentRef` (namespace defaults to the CR's own)

requirements.description: `hack/kind-config.yaml` SHALL declare an `extraPortMappings` entry mapping host TCP `443` to the Envoy proxy's fixed container NodePort on the control-plane node.
requirements.rationale: Without host→container port mapping, `https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3` cannot reach the Envoy proxy from the developer's machine.

scenarios.name: Mapping present on fresh clusters
scenarios.when: the kind cluster is recreated
scenarios.then: `docker port forge-e2e-control-plane` lists `443/tcp -> 0.0.0.0:443` (or the developer's chosen host port)
and_then: `curl -k https://127.0.0.1:443/` reaches Envoy and returns a TLS handshake for `keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io`

scenarios.name: Protocol and listenAddress explicit
scenarios.when: the `extraPortMappings` entry is written
scenarios.then: it declares `protocol: TCP` and `listenAddress: 0.0.0.0` (or `127.0.0.1` for belt-and-braces local-only)
and_then: The comment above the entry explains why 443 is exposed on kind only and points at CC-0088

scenarios.name: Backwards compatibility preserved
scenarios.when: an existing cluster created with the old config is re-used via `SKIP_KIND_CREATE=true`
scenarios.then: the script logs a WARN that the port mapping is missing and the Quick Start Gateway URL may not reach the proxy
and_then: The script does not hard-fail — the port-forward fallback still works

requirements.description: `hack/deploy-infra.sh` SHALL add a Gateway-readiness gate (Step 2b, or an addition to Step 4) that waits for `HelmRelease/envoy-gateway` Ready=True and `Gateway/openstack-gw` Programmed=True within `HELMRELEASE_TIMEOUT`.
requirements.rationale: Gates downstream steps so a broken Envoy Gateway install fails the deploy loudly with diagnostics, rather than silently leaving `https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3` unreachable.

scenarios.name: Wait succeeds on healthy cluster
scenarios.when: a fresh `make deploy-infra` runs to completion
scenarios.then: the new gate exits 0 once both Ready and Programmed reach True
and_then: Step 4 HelmRelease wait list additionally includes `envoy-gateway`

scenarios.name: Wait surfaces diagnostics on timeout
scenarios.when: the Envoy Gateway install never reaches Ready
scenarios.then: the script dumps `kubectl describe helmrelease/envoy-gateway`, `kubectl describe gateway/openstack-gw -n openstack`, and pod logs from `envoy-gateway-system` before exiting 1
and_then: The error message includes the observed `.status.conditions[].reason` for each resource

scenarios.name: Unit test shims kubectl
scenarios.when: `tests/unit/hack/deploy_infra_gateway_wait_test.sh` runs
scenarios.then: a fake `kubectl` shim feeds fake condition payloads and the function is verified to return 0 on True/True and non-zero on the negative cases
and_then: The test follows the pattern established by `deploy_infra_reconcile_sources_test.sh`

requirements.description: The Quick Start sample Keystone CR SHALL include `spec.gateway` with `parentRef.name: openstack-gw`, `hostname: keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io`, and `path: /` so that reconciliation produces `status.endpoint=https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3` and an HTTPRoute attached to `openstack-gw`.
requirements.rationale: Exercises the CC-0065 code path end-to-end from the on-ramp documentation.

scenarios.name: CR manifests match guide
scenarios.when: the user copies the Step 7 manifest verbatim
scenarios.then: `kubectl apply -f keystone.yaml` succeeds and the Keystone CR is admitted
and_then: `spec.gateway.parentRef.name == openstack-gw`
`spec.gateway.hostname == keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io`

scenarios.name: Status reflects gateway hostname
scenarios.when: reconciliation reaches DeploymentReady and HTTPRouteReady
scenarios.then: `status.endpoint == https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3`
and_then: `status.conditions[?(@.type=="HTTPRouteReady")].reason == "HTTPRouteAccepted"`

scenarios.name: Path default honored
scenarios.when: `spec.gateway.path: /` is set
scenarios.then: the resulting HTTPRoute has a `PathPrefix: /` match rule in `rules[0].matches`
and_then: The HTTPRoute `backendRefs[0].name == keystone-api` on port 5000 (the default Service)

requirements.description: The Quick Start `Access Keystone from your local machine` section SHALL be rewritten so that the primary flow uses `OS_AUTH_URL=https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3` with no `kubectl port-forward` and no `/etc/hosts` edit, while the existing port-forward instructions are preserved in a clearly labelled `Fallback` subsection.
requirements.rationale: Removes the two-terminal port-forward caveat and the catalog-URL limitation without breaking users on restricted networks.

scenarios.name: Primary flow uses nip.io URL
scenarios.when: a reader follows the rewritten section top-to-bottom
scenarios.then: they reach `openstack token issue` without running `kubectl port-forward svc/keystone-api`
and_then: The section includes the 1–2 sentence nip.io explainer
`curl -k https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3` is given as the smoke-test command

scenarios.name: Fallback subsection retained
scenarios.when: the reader scrolls further
scenarios.then: the `## Fallback — kubectl port-forward` subsection preserves the previous commands and explains when to use it (e.g. networks that block nip.io resolution)
and_then: The fallback links back to the primary path for normal use

scenarios.name: Self-signed TLS handling documented
scenarios.when: the reader reaches the `## Accept the self-signed certificate` subsection
scenarios.then: they are given two options: trust the CA extracted from `selfsigned-cluster-issuer` or pass `-k` / `OS_INSECURE=true`
and_then: The CA extraction commands are copy-pasteable and reference the `selfsigned-cluster-issuer` ClusterIssuer by name

requirements.description: `docs/quick-start.md` Step 3 `What happens` table, the architecture snapshot, and Step 7 sample CR SHALL be updated to reflect the Envoy Gateway install and the new `spec.gateway` block.
requirements.rationale: Consistency — all places that enumerate what the Quick Start deploys or how the sample CR looks must mention Envoy Gateway.

scenarios.name: Step 3 table lists Envoy Gateway
scenarios.when: the reader scans the `What happens` table
scenarios.then: a `Step 2b` row describes the Envoy Gateway + Gateway install between `Step 2a` (Gateway API CRDs) and `Step 3` (base overlay)
and_then: The row explains why `openstack-gw` lives in the `openstack` namespace

scenarios.name: Architecture snapshot updated
scenarios.when: the reader expands `What gets deployed`
scenarios.then: the code block includes `envoy-gateway-system   envoy-gateway-*   Ready`
and_then: No pre-existing rows are silently removed

scenarios.name: Step 7 manifest gains spec.gateway
scenarios.when: the reader views the sample CR
scenarios.then: the `spec.gateway` block is present with the three required fields
and_then: A `::: tip` or inline comment explains that the hostname is tied to nip.io and the Gateway in `openstack-gw`

requirements.description: A new Chainsaw E2E suite `tests/e2e/keystone/gateway-quick-start/` SHALL deploy a Keystone CR with `spec.gateway` against the real Envoy Gateway on kind and assert `HTTPRoute.status.parents[0].conditions[?(@.type=="Accepted")].status == True` from the real controller, without any manual status patching.
requirements.rationale: CI coverage for the real-controller path prevents regressions in Envoy Gateway, the EnvoyProxy NodePort config, and the operator's HTTPRoute reconciler from shipping.

scenarios.name: Real Accepted condition observed
scenarios.when: the suite applies the Keystone CR and waits
scenarios.then: `kubectl get httproute <name> -n openstack -o jsonpath='{.status.parents[0].conditions[?(@.type=="Accepted")].status}'` returns `True` within the Chainsaw assert timeout with no `kubectl patch --subresource=status` call in the test
and_then: The controllerName on the parent status matches `gateway.envoyproxy.io/gatewayclass-controller`

scenarios.name: status.endpoint validated
scenarios.when: reconciliation is complete
scenarios.then: the suite asserts `Keystone.status.endpoint == https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3`
and_then: The suite also asserts `HTTPRouteReady=True/HTTPRouteAccepted` on the CR

scenarios.name: Kind-only gating
scenarios.when: the suite is run via `chainsaw test --selector 'overlay!=kind'`
scenarios.then: it is skipped (label `overlay: kind` matches the `flux-web-health` precedent)
and_then: A runtime presence probe checks `gatewayclass/envoy` and exits 0 with `SKIP:` if absent

requirements.description: `renovate.json` SHALL gain a `customManagers` entry that extracts the Envoy Gateway chart version from `deploy/kind/base/envoy-gateway.yaml` and a matching `packageRules` entry with `minimumReleaseAge: "3 days"` and disabled major bumps.
requirements.rationale: Mirrors the `FLUX_OPERATOR_VERSION` pattern introduced by CC-0085 so chart bumps auto-PR with a cooldown.

scenarios.name: Version regex matches
scenarios.when: Renovate runs against `deploy/kind/base/envoy-gateway.yaml`
scenarios.then: the `customManagers` regex extracts `currentValue` and maps to `envoyproxy/gateway` (or equivalent) as `packageName`
and_then: `datasourceTemplate` is either `helm` or `github-releases` depending on chart distribution

scenarios.name: Major bumps disabled
scenarios.when: Renovate evaluates a major chart release
scenarios.then: no PR is opened until a maintainer manually bumps
and_then: The packageRule `enabled: false` for `matchUpdateTypes: ["major"]` is present

scenarios.name: Release-age buffer enforced
scenarios.when: Renovate evaluates a minor/patch chart release
scenarios.then: the PR is delayed by `minimumReleaseAge: "3 days"`
and_then: The groupName is set consistently (e.g. `envoy-gateway`)

requirements.description: Reference documentation (`docs/reference/infrastructure/e2e-deployment.md` and `docs/reference/keystone-crd.md`) SHALL be updated to surface the new Gateway install in the deployment diagram and to cross-link the CRD `spec.gateway` reference to the new Quick Start section.
requirements.rationale: Readers arrive at the CRD reference from many entry points; they need a pointer to a working kind example.

scenarios.name: e2e-deployment diagram updated
scenarios.when: the reader reaches `## Deployment Sequence`
scenarios.then: an `Install Envoy Gateway + Gateway/openstack-gw (kind-only)` block is rendered between the existing Gateway API CRD install and `Step 3`
and_then: The block notes that production overlays leave this install out

scenarios.name: CRD reference kind-specific note
scenarios.when: the reader opens the `Example — Basic Gateway Exposure` section
scenarios.then: a short admonition links to `docs/quick-start.md#access-keystone-from-your-local-machine` and clarifies `status.endpoint = https://{hostname}/v3` resolves on kind via the new addon
and_then: The admonition cites CC-0088 for future traceability

scenarios.name: No production promises introduced
scenarios.when: the reader is on the CRD reference
scenarios.then: existing wording that the Gateway/GatewayClass are infrastructure concerns outside the operator is preserved verbatim
and_then: No kind-specific guidance leaks into the operator's behavior contract

requirements.description: The production overlay (`deploy/flux-system/kustomization.yaml`, `deploy/flux-system/fluxinstance.yaml`, and `deploy/flux-system/releases/*`) SHALL NOT be modified by this feature; only `deploy/flux-system/sources/` MAY gain a new `envoy-gateway-charts.yaml` HelmRepository if Flux needs it as a shared source.
requirements.rationale: Production posture stays platform-agnostic — same rule enforced by CC-0082 and CC-0086. HelmRepository sources in `flux-system/sources/` are benign because they only declare *where* a chart can be fetched; no HelmRelease consumes the chart unless the kind overlay is applied.

scenarios.name: Diff is clean on production files
scenarios.when: `git diff main -- deploy/flux-system/kustomization.yaml deploy/flux-system/fluxinstance.yaml deploy/flux-system/releases/` runs after the feature lands
scenarios.then: the diff is empty
and_then: CI has a check (existing or new) that asserts this invariant, or the review criterion catches it

scenarios.name: Source-only addition allowed
scenarios.when: `deploy/flux-system/sources/envoy-gateway-charts.yaml` is added
scenarios.then: it contains only a `HelmRepository` object (no HelmRelease)
and_then: The source is referenced only from `deploy/kind/base/envoy-gateway.yaml`

scenarios.name: Alternative: reuse existing source
scenarios.when: the chart is available via an existing repo (e.g. oci://c5c3-charts) and no new source is needed
scenarios.then: no file is added under `deploy/flux-system/sources/`
and_then: The HelmRelease's `chart.spec.sourceRef` points at the pre-existing HelmRepository

requirements.description: The primary Quick Start Keystone access flow MAY be validated by the existing `tests/e2e/infrastructure/` suites (extension or new `gateway-quick-start-smoke` suite) to assert `curl -k https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3` returns HTTP 200 from inside the kind container via the host port mapping.
requirements.rationale: Defense-in-depth against a silent failure where the Gateway reaches Programmed but the host:443 → NodePort mapping breaks.

scenarios.name: HTTP 200 from inside test container
scenarios.when: the smoke test issues `curl -k https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3` from a pod on the node network
scenarios.then: it returns HTTP 200 with the Keystone version JSON
and_then: The body is non-empty and matches `{"version": {"id": "v3"`

scenarios.name: No port-forward used
scenarios.when: the smoke test runs
scenarios.then: it never calls `kubectl port-forward`
and_then: The test documents how nip.io resolves inside the kind container (using the host's DNS)

scenarios.name: Kind-only gating
scenarios.when: the suite is invoked on a non-kind overlay
scenarios.then: it is skipped with a `SKIP:` log line using the same presence-probe pattern as `flux-web-health`
and_then: The Chainsaw label `overlay: kind` is set

tasks.title: Decide chart source and add HelmRepository if needed (REQ-001, REQ-012) — either reuse existing oci://ghcr.io/c5c3/charts (if the chart is mirrored) or create deploy/flux-system/sources/envoy-gateway-charts.yaml with the upstream `oci://docker.io/envoyproxy/gateway-helm` (or https://gateway.envoyproxy.io-hosted repo). Source-only addition per REQ-012.

Create deploy/kind/base/envoy-gateway.yaml (REQ-001, REQ-002): Namespace `envoy-gateway-system`, `HelmRelease/envoy-gateway` referencing the source from 1.1 with a pinned version range, and an `EnvoyProxy` CR (apiVersion `gateway.envoyproxy.io/v1alpha1`) configuring the proxy Service as type `NodePort` with a fixed nodePort (e.g. 31443) for the HTTPS listener. Include SPDX header, feature-ID comment `CC-0088, REQ-001/REQ-002`.

Create deploy/kind/base/openstack-gateway.yaml (REQ-003): `GatewayClass/envoy` with controllerName `gateway.envoyproxy.io/gatewayclass-controller` and a `parametersRef` to the EnvoyProxy CR from 1.2; a cert-manager `Certificate` for `keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io` in namespace `openstack` referencing `selfsigned-cluster-issuer`; `Gateway/openstack-gw` in namespace `openstack` with a single HTTPS listener on port 443, hostname `keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io`, `tls.mode: Terminate` and `certificateRefs` to the Secret produced by the Certificate. SPDX header + `CC-0088, REQ-003`.

Wire the two new manifests into deploy/kind/base/kustomization.yaml (REQ-001): append `envoy-gateway.yaml` and `openstack-gateway.yaml` to `resources` beneath `headlamp.yaml`/`flux-web.yaml`. Do not touch `deploy/flux-system/kustomization.yaml` (REQ-012).

Update hack/kind-config.yaml (REQ-004): add a `nodes[0]` entry with `extraPortMappings` mapping `hostPort: 443` → `containerPort: 31443` (matching the fixed NodePort from 1.2) with `protocol: TCP` and `listenAddress: 127.0.0.1`. Comment references CC-0088.

Add renovate.json customManager + packageRules for the Envoy Gateway chart (REQ-010): new entry targeting `deploy/kind/base/envoy-gateway.yaml`, `minimumReleaseAge: "3 days"`, majors disabled, groupName `envoy-gateway`. Mirror the `flux-operator` block already present.

Extend hack/deploy-infra.sh (REQ-005): add `envoy-gateway` to the Phase-3 HelmRelease wait list and add a new `wait_for_gateway_programmed` helper polling `Gateway/openstack-gw -n openstack` for `Programmed=True`; call it after Phase 3. On timeout, dump `describe` of the Gateway and pod logs from `envoy-gateway-system`.

Add tests/unit/hack/deploy_infra_gateway_wait_test.sh (REQ-005): unit test covering the happy path (Programmed=True returns 0) and the timeout path (surfaces diagnostics and exits non-zero) via a fake `kubectl` shim. Follow `deploy_infra_reconcile_sources_test.sh` style and ensure the test is discovered by the existing Makefile target.
Add tests/unit/hack/kind_config_port_mapping_test.sh (REQ-004): uses `yq` to assert `.nodes[0].extraPortMappings[] | select(.hostPort==443)` exists with `containerPort: 31443` and `protocol: TCP`.

Add tests/unit/deploy/production_posture_test.sh (REQ-012): shell-script test ensuring `deploy/flux-system/kustomization.yaml`, `deploy/flux-system/fluxinstance.yaml`, and `deploy/flux-system/releases/*` are not modified relative to `origin/main` (skip when not in a git worktree) and that any added file under `deploy/flux-system/sources/` contains only HelmRepository objects.

Add tests/unit/renovate/envoy_gateway_manager_test.sh (REQ-010): validates `renovate.json` with `renovate-config-validator` if available, otherwise runs a Python regex-only test confirming the customManager regex extracts the chart version string from the fixture file and that the packageRule contains `minimumReleaseAge: 3 days` and major-disabled.

Update docs/quick-start.md Step 7 sample CR (REQ-006, REQ-008): insert `spec.gateway` with `parentRef.name: openstack-gw`, `hostname: keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io`, `path: /`; add a one-liner `::: tip` explaining the nip.io wildcard. Cite CC-0088 in the comment.

Rewrite docs/quick-start.md `## Access Keystone from your local machine` (REQ-007): primary flow uses `OS_AUTH_URL=https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3`; add `## Accept the self-signed certificate` subsection (CA extract or `-k`/`OS_INSECURE=true`); move the port-forward commands to `## Fallback — kubectl port-forward`.

Update docs/quick-start.md Step 3 `What happens` table and architecture snapshot (REQ-008): insert a `Step 2b` row for Envoy Gateway + `openstack-gw` install between `Step 2a` and `Step 3`; add `envoy-gateway-system   envoy-gateway-*   Ready` to the code-block snapshot. Note CC-0088.

Update docs/reference/infrastructure/e2e-deployment.md (REQ-011): insert an `Install Envoy Gateway + Gateway/openstack-gw (kind-only)` block in the ASCII deployment diagram between the existing `Install Gateway API standard CRDs` and `Step 3`. Note CC-0088 and that production overlays exclude this.

Update docs/reference/keystone-crd.md (REQ-011): add a kind-specific admonition near `### Example — Basic Gateway Exposure` linking to the new Quick Start section and clarifying that `status.endpoint = https://{hostname}/v3` actually resolves on a Quick Start cluster now. Cite CC-0088.

Add tests/unit/docs/quick_start_access_section_test.sh (REQ-007): asserts the primary `## Access Keystone from your local machine` section no longer contains `port-forward svc/keystone-api` before a `## Fallback` heading, that a nip.io explainer is present, and that an `## Accept the self-signed certificate` subsection exists.

Add tests/unit/docs/quick_start_coverage_test.sh and quick_start_sample_cr_test.sh (REQ-008): one asserts the Step 3 table gains the new row and the architecture snapshot is updated; the other extracts the Step 7 YAML block and verifies the three `spec.gateway` fields with `yq`.
Add tests/unit/docs/reference_cross_links_test.sh (REQ-011): markdown scan confirming the e2e-deployment diagram insertion and the keystone-crd admonition link + CC-0088 citation.

Create tests/e2e/keystone/gateway-quick-start/ Chainsaw suite (REQ-006, REQ-009): chainsaw-test.yaml + fixtures (00-namespace.yaml, 01-keystone-cr.yaml). Label `overlay: kind` + presence probe skipping on non-kind. Assert HTTPRoute Accepted=True from real controller, `status.endpoint`, `HTTPRouteReady=True/HTTPRouteAccepted`. No manual status patches.

Add Gateway-programmed assertion to the gateway-quick-start suite (REQ-003): chainsaw step asserting `Gateway/openstack-gw` `Programmed=True` and listener `Accepted=True`, plus Certificate Ready assertion.

Add EnvoyProxy NodePort assertion to the suite (REQ-002): shell step using `kubectl get svc -l gateway.envoyproxy.io/owning-gateway-name=openstack-gw -o json` + jq to confirm `type=NodePort` and the declared nodePort.

Add tests/e2e/infrastructure/gateway-quick-start-smoke/chainsaw-test.yaml (REQ-013): from a kind-internal pod (or CI host), `curl -k https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3` returns HTTP 200 with non-empty Keystone JSON. Mirror flux-web-health's label gating + presence probe; CC-0088 feature comment.

Wire the new e2e suites into the Makefile + CI (REQ-009, REQ-013): ensure `make e2e` discovers `tests/e2e/keystone/gateway-quick-start/` and `tests/e2e/infrastructure/gateway-quick-start-smoke/`; update any per-suite list in `tests/e2e/chainsaw-config.yaml` if required.

Cross-check: re-read issue #265 acceptance criteria (12 items) against each doc task, manifest task, and test task; fill any gap immediately. Produce a short mapping table of acceptance-criterion → task-ID in the PR description.

Run a full local `make deploy-infra` + `make e2e` dry run against a fresh kind cluster (REQ-001..REQ-009, REQ-013): capture timings, confirm every acceptance criterion is satisfied on a clean box, and update the PR description with smoke-test output.

test_specifications.story: Kind-only Envoy Gateway install reconciles through Flux without touching the production overlay (CC-0088)
test_specifications.expected: [e2e] `HelmRelease/envoy-gateway -n envoy-gateway-system` reaches `Ready=True` within HELMRELEASE_TIMEOUT on a fresh kind cluster

test_specifications.story: Kind-only Envoy Gateway install reconciles through Flux without touching the production overlay (CC-0088)
test_specifications.expected: [e2e] Diff check verifies `deploy/flux-system/kustomization.yaml`, `deploy/flux-system/fluxinstance.yaml`, and `deploy/flux-system/releases/*` are unchanged vs. main

test_specifications.story: Quick Start user reaches Keystone at a stable HTTPS URL without a port-forward (CC-0088)
test_specifications.expected: [e2e] The Envoy proxy Service is of type NodePort and the HTTPS listener port matches the fixed nodePort declared in the EnvoyProxy CR

test_specifications.story: Quick Start user reaches Keystone at a stable HTTPS URL without a port-forward (CC-0088)
test_specifications.expected: [unit] `yq` parses `hack/kind-config.yaml` and confirms an `extraPortMappings` entry maps host 443 → container NodePort with protocol TCP

test_specifications.story: Keystone CRD `spec.gateway` code path is exercised end-to-end on the Quick Start cluster (CC-0088)
test_specifications.expected: [e2e] `Gateway/openstack-gw -n openstack` reaches `Programmed=True` with all listeners `Accepted=True`

test_specifications.story: Kind-only Envoy Gateway install reconciles through Flux without touching the production overlay (CC-0088)
test_specifications.expected: [e2e] cert-manager `Certificate` for `keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io` reaches `Ready=True` and the referenced TLS Secret exists with `tls.crt`+`tls.key`

test_specifications.story: Deployment script waits for Gateway readiness and fails fast on regressions (CC-0088)
test_specifications.expected: [unit] Fake kubectl shim returns `.status.conditions[?(@.type=="Programmed")].status = True` and the new wait function returns 0

test_specifications.story: Deployment script waits for Gateway readiness and fails fast on regressions (CC-0088)
test_specifications.expected: [unit] Shim returns False indefinitely; function exits non-zero and prints `kubectl describe gateway/openstack-gw` + Envoy Gateway pod logs

test_specifications.story: Keystone CRD `spec.gateway` code path is exercised end-to-end on the Quick Start cluster (CC-0088)
test_specifications.expected: [e2e] After reconciliation, `Keystone.status.endpoint == https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3`

test_specifications.story: Keystone CRD `spec.gateway` code path is exercised end-to-end on the Quick Start cluster (CC-0088)
test_specifications.expected: [e2e] Operator-managed HTTPRoute has PathPrefix `/`, backendRef `keystone-api:5000`, and parentRef `openstack-gw`

test_specifications.story: Quick Start reader understands how nip.io and self-signed TLS work (CC-0088)
test_specifications.expected: [unit] Markdown scan confirms the primary `## Access Keystone from your local machine` section no longer contains `port-forward svc/keystone-api` before the `## Fallback` subsection, and the `## Accept the self-signed certificate` subsection is present

test_specifications.story: Quick Start reader understands how nip.io and self-signed TLS work (CC-0088)
test_specifications.expected: [unit] Markdown scan confirms a 1–2 sentence nip.io explainer referencing `*.127-0-0-1.nip.io` → `127.0.0.1` is present

test_specifications.expected: [unit] Markdown scan confirms a `Step 2b` row for Envoy Gateway exists in the `What happens` table and the architecture snapshot includes `envoy-gateway-system   envoy-gateway-*   Ready`

review_criteria: BLOCKER: All SHALL requirements (REQ-001..REQ-012) have at least one passing test specification linked by requirement_id; no acceptance criterion from issue #265 is unrepresented in the task list

BLOCKER: `git diff main -- deploy/flux-system/kustomization.yaml deploy/flux-system/fluxinstance.yaml deploy/flux-system/releases/` is empty after the PR; the production-posture unit test (REQ-012) passes

BLOCKER: On a fresh kind cluster, `curl -k https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3` returns HTTP 200 with Keystone v3 JSON without any port-forward; `openstack token issue` succeeds against the nip.io URL

BLOCKER: `tests/e2e/keystone/gateway-quick-start/` asserts `HTTPRoute.status.parents[0].conditions[?(@.type=="Accepted")].status == True` from the real Envoy Gateway controller (no manual status patches anywhere in the suite)

CRITICAL: The primary Quick Start `## Access Keystone from your local machine` section no longer references `port-forward svc/keystone-api` before the `## Fallback` heading; the `## Accept the self-signed certificate` subsection is present and documents both trust-the-CA and -k/OS_INSECURE options

CRITICAL: `hack/deploy-infra.sh` waits for `HelmRelease/envoy-gateway` Ready and `Gateway/openstack-gw` Programmed=True with diagnostic dumps on timeout; shell-level unit tests cover both happy and timeout paths

CRITICAL: `renovate.json` customManager regex extracts the Envoy Gateway chart version and the matching packageRule sets `minimumReleaseAge: "3 days"` with majors disabled — validated by `renovate-config-validator` or a regex unit test
CRITICAL: Gateway/openstack-gw lives in namespace `openstack`, same as the Keystone CR — no ReferenceGrant is required or introduced
CRITICAL: cert-manager Certificate Ready=True and the referenced Secret contains `tls.crt`+`tls.key` before the Gateway is considered ready in the E2E suite
MINOR: All new files carry the SPDX-FileCopyrightText + SPDX-License-Identifier header used by the rest of the repo and a CC-0088 feature-ID citation in their header comment
MINOR: The `flux-web-health` gating pattern (`overlay: kind` label + runtime presence probe + `SKIP:` log line) is mirrored in both new E2E suites so non-kind overlays pass cleanly
MINOR: The existing `tests/e2e/keystone/network-policy/` suite still passes after the real Envoy Gateway install — namespaceSelector behaviour is unchanged
MINOR: Docs carry consistent anchors and cross-links; the CRD reference admonition, the e2e-deployment diagram insert, and the Quick Start Step 3 table all cite CC-0088
implementation_notes:
ARCHITECTURE

Component topology (kind-only):

    ┌────────────── developer host ──────────────┐
    │  curl https://keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io/v3 │
    │   ↓ public DNS: *.127-0-0-1.nip.io → 127.0.0.1 │
    │   ↓ TCP :443 (host)                        │
    └───────────────── ↓ ────────────────────────┘
                      │ kind extraPortMappings 443→31443
    ┌──────────── kind control-plane container ───────────┐
    │  NodePort Service (Envoy data plane) :31443         │
    │    ↓                                                │
    │  envoy-gateway-system: envoy-proxy-* pod            │
    │    ↓  HTTPS listener :443 (TLS terminate, SNI keystone.127-0-0-1.nip.io) │
    │    ↓  attached via Gateway/openstack-gw             │
    │  openstack: HTTPRoute/keystone-api (PathPrefix /)   │
    │    ↓                                                │
    │  openstack: Service/keystone-api :5000 → pods       │
    └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Deploy-time control flow:

  make deploy-infra
    └─ hack/deploy-infra.sh
         Step 1: create kind cluster (uses new extraPortMappings)
         Step 2:  flux-operator + FluxInstance
         Step 2a: Gateway API standard CRDs
         Step 3:  kubectl apply -k deploy/kind/base → HelmRepository, HelmRelease/envoy-gateway, GatewayClass, Certificate, Gateway
         Step 4:  wait_for_helmreleases ... envoy-gateway (added)
                  + wait_for_gateway_programmed openstack-gw openstack (new)
         Step 5..8: unchanged

Crucial invariants:
  - Gateway/openstack-gw lives in `openstack` namespace, matching the Keystone CR → no ReferenceGrant required (operator does not manage ReferenceGrant; keystone_types.go:348-354).
  - EnvoyProxy CR is the only supported way to pin NodePort for the data-plane Service; MetalLB is explicitly out of scope.
  - status.endpoint is derived by the operator as `https://{hostname}/v3` when spec.gateway is set; the nip.io hostname is the single source of truth in the sample CR.
  - Production overlay must stay byte-identical (REQ-012 + diff-based unit test). Source-only additions under deploy/flux-system/sources/ are benign because no production HelmRelease consumes them.
  - Renovate customManager + packageRule pair mirrors the `flux-operator` precedent from CC-0085 (renovate.json:32-44).

Patterns to follow:
  - Header comment convention from deploy/kind/base/headlamp.yaml and flux-web.yaml explaining `kind-only — never production` with rationale + CC-0088 feature ID.
  - Chainsaw suite gating via `overlay: kind` label + runtime presence probe, mirroring tests/e2e/infrastructure/flux-web-health/.
  - Deploy-script unit tests via a fake kubectl shim, mirroring tests/unit/hack/deploy_infra_reconcile_sources_test.sh.

Pitfalls & how to avoid them:
  - Binding privileged port 443 can fail on restricted CI. Use `listenAddress: 127.0.0.1` and document how to change the host port if needed.
  - Chart version drift can break EnvoyProxy schema. Pin via renovate with 3-day cooldown and majors disabled.
  - nip.io DNS is third-party — keep the port-forward Fallback documented and gate REQ-013 on presence probe so non-network environments skip cleanly.
  - cert-manager Certificate DNS name mismatch is a silent failure mode; the E2E suite MUST assert Certificate Ready=True before moving on.
  - Network-policy E2E (tests/e2e/keystone/network-policy/) continues to reference envoy-gateway-system by namespaceSelector; the presence of a real Envoy Gateway install must not change that test's outcome — verify with a post-merge run.

Data flow summary:
  spec.gateway.hostname  →  HTTPRoute.spec.hostnames (operator)
                          →  Gateway listener hostname match (envoy-gateway)
                          →  Envoy proxy TLS SNI match
                          →  routes to HTTPRoute backendRef: keystone-api:5000
                          →  Keystone.status.endpoint = https://{hostname}/v3 (operator)

No operator code changes are required; all wiring is manifest + script + docs + tests.
